serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Butter Prawns in Singapore is approximately 150g, representing a shared side dish portion of 4–6 whole prawns coated in creamy butter sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Butter Prawns — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Butter Prawns (cr_id G00011053). All per_100g macros and available micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the butter and evaporated milk content profile as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. Detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB record for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Butter Prawns as a composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood and sauce dishes with moderate carbohydrate content (7.9g/100g from evaporated milk and minor starch). The protein content (13.2g/100g) and fat content (1.2g/100g) significantly blunt postprandial glucose response. GL per 150g serving = (45 × 7.92 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.3g. Analogous reference: mixed prawn dish with sauce, GI ~40–50, University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 502mg/100g with insufficient potassium offset (184mg/100g, below the 200mg/100g threshold)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Butter Prawns to small portions (≤100g) and request reduced salt and butter in preparation, as sodium at 502mg/100g accumulates rapidly and potassium at 184mg/100g is insufficient to offset the sodium load under standard dietary offset criteria.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.9g/100g), low sugar (3.0g/100g), high protein (13.2g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter Prawns is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as the low carbohydrate content (7.9g/100g), low sugar (3.0g/100g), and high protein (13.2g/100g) support glycaemic stability; however, portion size should be monitored as butter and evaporated milk contribute caloric density and saturated fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (502mg/100g) and butter-derived saturated fat; cholesterol at 119mg/100g from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Butter Prawns sparingly due to the elevated sodium content (502mg/100g), butter-derived saturated fat, and prawn cholesterol (119mg/100g); substituting butter with olive oil and reducing sauce volume can meaningfully lower the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Butter as primary cooking fat (pro-inflammatory saturated dairy fat); dried chillies are nightshades
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Butter Prawns is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to butter's pro-inflammatory saturated dairy fat profile and the presence of dried chillies (nightshades), which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil would substantially improve the anti-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Prawns is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian seafood dish consisting of whole prawns stir-fried or wok-cooked in a rich, creamy sauce made from butter, evaporated milk, egg yolk, curry leaves, and dried chillies. The dish is characterised by its golden, frothy, aromatic sauce that coats each prawn, delivering a savoury, mildly sweet, and slightly spicy flavour profile with a rich, creamy texture. It is commonly served as a shared side dish at Chinese-style seafood restaurants and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, typically accompanied by steamed white rice. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 99 kcal, 13.2g protein, 1.2g fat, 7.9g carbohydrate, 2.96g sugar, 0.4g dietary fibre, and 502mg sodium. The high protein content from prawns supports satiety and glycaemic stability, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. However, the elevated sodium content (502mg/100g) and butter-derived saturated fat raise concerns for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The dish contains crustacean, dairy (butter, evaporated milk), and egg allergens. Curry leaves contribute mild anti-inflammatory micronutrients, while dried chillies add capsaicin. The dish is halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified ingredients.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Butter Prawns (cr_id G00011053) with a stated confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ingredient composition as HPB does not separately report this field for the dish. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), full vitamin panel (C, A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, D, E), phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite Butter Prawns dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id G00011053) as of 2026. The HPB Foodo database was consulted as the primary approved source and confirmed that these fields are not available for this dish entry. This is consistent with limited published micronutrient data for traditional composite preparations in the HPB database. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 0.5g/100g based on the butter and evaporated milk content profile in the dish formulation; this is a conservative estimate as the HPB record reports total fat at only 1.22g/100g, suggesting a relatively low butter-to-prawn ratio in the HPB-measured preparation. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed seafood sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Butter Prawns exists in approved sources. The input record listed fat_g as 1.22g/100g which is notably low for a butter-based dish, suggesting the HPB measurement may reflect a specific lower-fat preparation variant; this value is preserved as sourced from the canonical HPB record. Hindi local name corrected to Devanagari script: मक्खन झींगा.
